straight_beat.name: Lars Knutson
straight_beat.column:
The Twins didn’t just beat the Red Sox yesterday—they outplayed them, outpitched them, and outmaneuvered them in a way that makes you think maybe this team is finally starting to figure out how to win consistently. Six runs, zero errors, and a bullpen that didn’t fold under the pressure. It wasn’t the most thrilling game, but it was the kind of performance that makes you lean forward in your chair and say, “Okay, this might be it.”  

The pitching staff, which has been a question mark all season, looked like a unit for once. Joe Ryan, who’s been walking too many batters and missing too many swings, settled down after the first inning. He threw 70 pitches, allowed just two hits, and struck out five. You could see the difference in his demeanor—no more flinching at the first pitch, no more second-guessing his location. The front office might want to trade for a starter, but maybe they don’t have to. Ryan’s got a chance to be the guy who keeps this team in games.  

The offense, meanwhile, didn’t need to do much. They didn’t hit a lot of homers, didn’t steal bases, but they did the basics: drive in runs, move runners over, and capitalize on opportunities. The key was the defense, which held Boston to a single hit and two walks. You could argue that the Twins’ pitching staff has been the problem all year, but yesterday, they looked like a team that knows how to protect leads.  

But here’s the thing: this win doesn’t solve everything. The Twins are still 2.

optimist.name: Marlene Holmqvist
optimist.column:
The Twins took down the Red Sox yesterday, 6-0, and if you’re looking for a reason to believe this could be the year, you don’t have to look far. The ballclub’s got that playoff feel about it, like a Sunday morning service—everyone’s in their seats, the music’s got a rhythm, and the crowd’s got that hush before the first note. Buxton’s knees feel good this week and that’s all the forecast I need. Minnesota nice is about to turn into Minnesota nasty, mark it down.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: 11-7, two-and-a-half games back in the AL Central. That’s a lot of games left, and the standings are a cruel teacher. But here’s the thing—this team’s got a way of turning the page. They’ve got

pessimist.name: Gunnar 'Gunny' Bjornstad
pessimist.column:
The Twins rolled past the Red Sox yesterday, 6-0, and for a moment there, it felt like the old days. You know, the days when a win was a win and the standings didn’t feel like a spreadsheet filled with red ink. But here we are, 11-7, three games behind the Central Division lead, and the Pohlads are already telling me it’s different. It isn’t.  

Let’s not pretend this was a statement game. The Red Sox came in with a rotation that’s been a sieve since March, and the Twins’ offense didn’t exactly light up the scoreboard. Six runs? That’s not exactly a landslide. It’s the kind of performance that makes you think, “Okay, maybe we’re trending upward,” until you remember the last time they did that. Last October, when they blinked out of the playoffs like a bulb flickering on and off.  

The front office has been busy, of course. Trade deadline deals, free agent signings, all that jazz. But here’s the thing: you can’t build a team by pretending the calendar page is a blank slate. The Twins have been in this position before—2022, 2021, 2019—and every time, the magic fades by April. The Pohlads run a bakery and the bread’s always stale. Every October they’re gone in three games and every April they’re telling me it’s different. It isn’t.  

Look, I
